era_context: The early 18th century saw the consolidation of European great-power politics following the War of Spanish Succession (1701–1714). The Russian Empire under Peter I was expanding militarily and culturally; the Ottoman Empire remained a major power but faced increasing pressure; China under the Kangxi Emperor was at the height of Qing prosperity; and colonial competition for trade and territory was intensifying globally.

events.title: Pruth River Campaign: Peter I's Ottoman War
events.region: Ottoman Empire, Moldavia, Wallachia, Russian Empire
events.description: Peter I of Russia launched a campaign against the Ottoman Empire, hoping to capture Ottoman territories in the Balkans and secure access to the Black Sea. The campaign culminated in July 1711 at the Pruth River, where Peter's army was surrounded by Ottoman forces under Grand Vizier Köprülü Numan Pasha. Facing encirclement and supply exhaustion, Peter negotiated the Treaty of the Pruth, which forced Russia to cede Azov and dismantle fortifications, a significant setback for Russian expansion southward. The campaign demonstrated the limits of Russian military power against the Ottoman Empire at this time.
key_figures: Catherine I (Peter's wife, played diplomatic role)

sources.name: Peter I's correspondence and military records
Historiographic consensus on the Pruth Campaign

events.certainty_note: The campaign and treaty are extensively documented in both Russian and Ottoman primary sources, with precise dating to July 1711.
cross_references: Leads to 1721-001 (Treaty of Nystad, Russian expansion northward instead)

events.title: Tuscarora War: Indigenous Uprising in North Carolina
events.description: The Tuscarora Nation, facing encroachment by English settlers, enslavement of their people, and land dispossession, launched a coordinated uprising against colonial settlements in North Carolina. Led by Chief Hancock and other leaders, the Tuscarora attacked plantations and settlements in September 1711, killing approximately 200 colonists. The uprising was a response to decades of colonial expansion and exploitation. Colonial militias and allied Native American groups (including the Yamasee) eventually suppressed the rebellion by 1713, leading to the near-destruction of Tuscarora power in the region and the forced migration of survivors northward to join the Haudenosaunee Confederacy.
key_figures: Chief Tom Blount (Tuscarora, later negotiated peace)
John Lawson (colonial surveyor, killed in the uprising)

sources.name: Colonial records and militia reports from North Carolina
John Lawson's 'A New Voyage to Carolina' (published 1709, but accounts of the war added posthumously)
Oral traditions preserved in Haudenosaunee and Tuscarora communities

events.certainty_note: The uprising is well-documented in colonial records with precise dating to September 1711. Casualty figures and motivations are confirmed across multiple sources.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of colonial-indigenous conflict in early 18th-century America
Leads to 1713-001 (Treaty of Portsmouth, end of Tuscarora War)

events.title: South Sea Company Founded
events.description: The South Sea Company was chartered by the English Parliament as a joint-stock company with a monopoly on English trade to the South Seas (the Pacific coast of South America). The company was granted a loan to the government in exchange for exclusive trading rights and government support. Initially intended to facilitate trade and colonial expansion, the company became a vehicle for financial speculation and would later become infamous for the South Sea Bubble of 1720. In 1711, however, the company represented optimism about colonial commerce and the power of joint-stock enterprises.
key_figures: Robert Harley (Earl of Oxford, chief architect)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on early modern joint-stock companies

events.certainty_note: The founding of the South Sea Company is precisely documented in 1711 parliamentary records.
cross_references: Part of broader expansion of English colonial and commercial enterprise

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's Southern Inspection Tour
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor undertook his fourth major inspection tour of southern China, focusing on the economically vital Jiangnan region (Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces). These tours were designed to demonstrate imperial authority, inspect public works (particularly the Grand Canal and dikes), and gather intelligence on local conditions. The 1711 tour reinforced Kangxi's image as a benevolent ruler concerned with the welfare of his subjects and the maintenance of critical infrastructure. Such tours were significant expressions of Qing imperial ideology and governance.

sources.name: Kangxi's imperial edicts and travel records
Official Qing court records and memorials

events.certainty_note: Kangxi's inspection tours are extensively documented in Qing imperial records and local sources.
cross_references: Part of Kangxi's broader governance strategy (r. 1661–1722)
Reflects Qing consolidation of control over southern China

events.title: Handel's 'Rinaldo' Premieres in London
events.description: George Frideric Handel's opera 'Rinaldo' premiered at the Queen's Theatre in London on 24 April 1711. It was Handel's first opera written for the English stage and became an immediate success, establishing him as a major figure in English musical life. The opera featured elaborate staging, including live birds on stage, and drew on the Italian operatic tradition while appealing to English audiences. 'Rinaldo' marked a turning point in Handel's career and contributed to the establishment of Italian opera as a fashionable entertainment in England.
events.certainty_note: The premiere date and details are precisely documented in theatre records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of Handel's broader career in England (1710–1759)
Reflects the growth of Italian opera in England during the early 18th century

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Pruth Campaign has been somewhat reassessed by modern scholarship: while traditionally portrayed as a major Russian defeat, some historians argue that Peter's negotiated exit, though costly, prevented a more catastrophic encirclement and allowed Russia to redirect resources to the Great Northern War, which ultimately proved more strategically important. The Tuscarora War is sometimes presented in older colonial narratives as a 'rebellion' without adequate context for the systematic dispossession and enslavement that provoked it; modern scholarship emphasizes the colonial causes rather than portraying it as an unprovoked uprising.
historiographic_note: The year 1711 is well-documented in European and East Asian sources but relies heavily on official records and elite perspectives. Colonial American sources are abundant but often reflect colonial biases against indigenous peoples. Ottoman sources exist but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The year benefits from rich documentation across multiple regions, though the interpretation of events (particularly regarding causation and significance) varies by historiographic tradition.

graph_edges.note: The Pruth Campaign's failure redirected Russian expansion northward, contributing to the Great Northern War's conclusion and Russian gains in the Baltic.
The Tuscarora uprising led to colonial military campaigns that effectively ended Tuscarora independence in the Carolinas by 1713.
The South Sea Company's founding set the stage for the speculative bubble of 1720.
